summary: Venezuelan general and revolutionary (1783-1830) nicknamed "El Libertador" ("the Liberator") who led the independence movements of Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia (named for him) from Spanish rule. After early setbacks and exile in Jamaica, where he wrote the influential "Jamaica Letter," he won Venezuela back in the 1813 Admirable Campaign, secured New Granada's independence at the Battle of Boyaca (1819), founded the confederation of Gran Colombia as its first president, and finished liberating Venezuela at the Battle of Carabobo (1821). His final years were consumed by disputes with Francisco de Paula Santander and the collapse of Gran Colombia; he died in 1830. He is the namesake of Bolivia, Venezuela's currency, and Hugo Chavez's "Bolivarian Revolution," and is the protagonist of Gabriel Garcia Marquez's novel The General in His Labyrinth.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Bolivar is identified in giveaways as the Venezuelan general nicknamed "El Libertador" who drove Spain out of much of northern South America, became Gran Colombia's first president, and worked alongside Jose de San Martin to liberate much of the continent. He is credited with the independence of five countries, including Bolivia, which is named for him.

works.name: Francisco de Miranda & Early Revolutionary Career
works.description: Bolivar's early revolutionary career followed that of the older Venezuelan revolutionary Francisco de Miranda, nicknamed "The Precursor," who had spent years canvassing European courts for support of Spanish American independence. A youthful vow Bolivar made while visiting Rome set him on his path, and his eventual encounter with Miranda is commemorated today by a plaque in London.

examples: This man's career followed that of The Precursor, Francisco de Miranda.

works.name: The Jamaica Letter & Exile
works.description: After the fall of the Second Venezuelan Republic to Jose Tomas Boves's royalist "Legion of Hell," Bolivar fled into exile on the British colony of Jamaica, where in 1815 he wrote the "Jamaica Letter" (Carta de Jamaica) to the merchant Henry Cullen, arguing for the necessity of Spanish American union. The disillusioning failures of the earlier "Patria Boba" period pushed him toward a more conservative political outlook reflected in the letter.

clues.clue: Wrote the "Jamaica Letter" (1815) to the merchant Henry Cullen, arguing for the necessity of Spanish American unity

examples: A letter by this leader lists only six "sure conclusions" including that "if it were possible for any part of the world to revert to primitive chaos, it would be [their country] in its last hour".

clues.clue: Defeated by the royalist caudillo Jose Tomas Boves and his "Legion of Hell," which brought down the Second Republic of Venezuela

works.name: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death
works.description: In 1813, preceded by his Cartagena Manifesto, Bolivar led the Admirable Campaign, a rapid march from Cartagena through New Granada that recaptured Venezuela and ended in the capture of Caracas. During it he issued the notorious "Decree of War to the Death," sanctioning the killing of Spanish resistance, and received the dog Nevado, which would accompany him for years.

works.name: The Battle of Boyaca & the Congress of Cucuta
works.description: After a daring crossing of the Andes, Bolivar and Francisco de Paula Santander won the decisive Battle of Boyaca in August 1819, securing New Granada's independence and leading to the founding of Gran Colombia. The Congress of Cucuta later drafted the Constitution of Cucuta (also called the Constitution of 1821), Gran Colombia's founding document, which remained in force until the confederation's collapse in 1831.

works.name: The Battle of Carabobo & Liberation of Venezuela
works.description: Bolivar completed Venezuela's liberation at the Battle of Carabobo (1821), defeating the Royalist army of Miguel de la Torre with the help of his chief subordinate Jose Antonio Paez, aide-de-camp Daniel O'Leary, and horsemen called llaneros — though his beloved dog Nevado was killed in the fighting.

works.name: The Guayaquil Conference & Liberation of Peru and Bolivia
works.description: Bolivar met fellow liberator Jose de San Martin at the Guayaquil Conference; his refusal to share command there caused San Martin to lose momentum and withdraw, leaving Bolivar to become dictator of Peru. Alongside his lieutenant Antonio Jose de Sucre, he then won independence for Bolivia, the country later named for him.

works.name: Presidency, the Ocana Convention & Collapse of Gran Colombia
works.description: As president of Gran Colombia, Bolivar proposed a never-implemented tricameral legislature and increasingly clashed with Francisco de Paula Santander over presidential power. After a 1828 constitutional convention at Ocana (which he skipped, sending Daniel O'Leary as proxy) collapsed when the Santander faction walked out, Bolivar declared himself dictator; Gran Colombia dissolved in 1831, the year after his death and the assassination of his close friend Antonio Jose de Sucre.

works.name: Manuela Saenz
works.description: Manuela Saenz was Bolivar's longtime lover and closest confidante, whom he met in Quito after liberating the Viceroyalty of New Granada. A revolutionary in her own right, she spied for the independence movement, may have fought at the Battle of Ayacucho, and famously saved Bolivar from assassination during the 1828 Septembrine Conspiracy, earning the nickname "Libertadora del Libertador."

works.name: Legacy: Modern "Bolivarianism" & Namesakes
works.description: Bolivar's name and legacy pervade modern Venezuelan politics: Hugo Chavez and Nicolas Maduro's brand of socialism, the renamed "Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela," its social "missions," and the ALBA trade bloc all invoke him. He is also namesake of Bolivia and its currency, Venezuela's currency, and numerous streets, squares, hotels, and monuments across the Americas.

The Orinoco flows past this city of east-central Venezuela, the capital and namesake of its largest state by area. This city was founded as Angostura before being renamed in 1846.

Pietro Tenerani sculpted a statue of this person for a namesake square that contains Lievano Palace in La Candelaria.

works.name: Legacy: Literary Depictions
works.description: Bolivar is the title character of Gabriel Garcia Marquez's 1989 novel The General in His Labyrinth, which depicts his final, physically deteriorating river journey down the Magdalena and draws partly on his own preserved late writings. He also appears in several other Latin American novels and has been quoted in works ranging from a Marie Arana biography to John Green's Looking for Alaska.

clues.clue: Title character/protagonist of Gabriel Garcia Marquez's 1989 novel The General in His Labyrinth, depicting his final days on a journey down the Magdalena River
examples: name this protagonist of Gabriel Garcia Marquez's The General in His Labyrinth, the real-life author of the "Jamaica letter" and first president of Gran Colombia.
This man rides, Christ-like, into several towns on a mule and sleeps with Queen Marie Louise in a novel in which he travels along the Magdalena River.

clues.clue: The novel drew on his own late writings, preserved because Daniel O'Leary refused to burn them; in it, he learns Rafael Urdaneta has taken over the government and that Sucre has been killed
examples: Since Daniel O'Leary refused to burn this leader's late writings, an author was able to use them as a source for a 1989 novel depicting his last days, in which he learns that Rafael Urdaneta has taken over government and his friend Sucre has been killed.
In that novel, he writes many letters to his longtime lover Manuela Saenz and flies into a rage upon hearing of the assassination of Sucre during his journey to flee to Europe.

examples: This man's enemies jeer at him by yelling "Sausage link!" in another novel, in which he is treated by the eccentric Hercules Gastelbondo.
In that novel, he has an extended conversation with Diocles Atlantique in which he exclaims "let us have our Middle Ages in peace!"

examples: This man's mistress is the subject of Denzil Romero's erotic novel The Wife of Dr. Thorne.

clues.clue: In John Green's Looking for Alaska, the character Pudge finds part of a line spoken by him, "straight and fast is the only way out," written in the margins
examples: In John Green's Looking for Alaska, Pudge finds part of a line spoken by this man, "straight and fast is the only way out," written in the margins.

clues.clue: His memoirist Henri Ducoudray Holstein, in memoirs about fighting alongside him, describes him as a coward, a lecher, and incompetent
examples: In his memoirs about fighting with man, Henri Ducoudray Holstein describes him as a coward, a lecher, and incompetent.

comprehensive_summary: Simon Bolivar (1783-1830) was a Venezuelan general and revolutionary nicknamed "El Libertador" who led the independence of Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia (which is named for him) from Spanish rule, working at times alongside fellow liberator Jose de San Martin. His early revolutionary career followed that of the older Venezuelan revolutionary Francisco de Miranda, "The Precursor"; a youthful vow made while visiting Rome set him on his path, and his later encounter with Miranda is commemorated by a plaque at 58 Grafton Way in London. After the royalist caudillo Jose Tomas Boves and his "Legion of Hell" crushed the Second Republic of Venezuela, Bolivar fled into exile on Jamaica, where in 1815 he wrote the "Jamaica Letter" to the merchant Henry Cullen, arguing for Spanish American unity and reflecting a more conservative outlook shaped by the failures of the earlier "Patria Boba" period. In 1813, preceded by his Cartagena Manifesto, he had already led the Admirable Campaign, a rapid march from Cartagena through New Granada — with victories at Ocana, Cucuta, Niquitao, and Los Horcones aided by the guide Tinjaca — that recaptured Caracas; during it he issued the notorious "Decree of War to the Death" and received his dog Nevado, while Colonel Girardot was killed planting a flag on Barbula Hill. After his Jamaica exile, Bolivar and Francisco de Paula Santander won the decisive Battle of Boyaca (August 1819) following a daring crossing of the Andes, securing New Granada's independence and leading to the founding of Gran Colombia, whose Constitution of Cucuta was drafted by the Congress of Cucuta and remained in force until 1831. He completed Venezuela's liberation at the Battle of Carabobo (1821), defeating Miguel de la Torre's Royalist army with the help of his subordinate Jose Antonio Paez (the "Centaur of the Plains"), aide-de-camp Daniel O'Leary, and llanero cavalry who nicknamed him "iron buttocks" for his horseback endurance — though his dog Nevado was killed in the battle. He then met San Martin at the Guayaquil Conference; his refusal to share command caused San Martin to withdraw, leaving Bolivar to become dictator of Peru and, with his lieutenant Antonio Jose de Sucre, to win Bolivia's independence, becoming its first president. As president of Gran Colombia, Bolivar proposed a never-implemented tricameral legislature and increasingly clashed with Santander; after an 1828 constitutional convention at Ocana (which he skipped, sending O'Leary as proxy) collapsed when the Santander faction walked out, he declared himself dictator. He survived an 1828 assassination attempt, the Septembrine Conspiracy, thanks to his longtime lover Manuela Saenz, whom he had met in Quito after liberating the Viceroyalty of New Granada; she led the plotters on a wild goose chase while he escaped out a window, earning the nickname "Libertadora del Libertador." A revolutionary in her own right, Saenz received Peru's Order of the Sun, was known as the "Dame of the Sun," may have fought at the Battle of Ayacucho, and later met Giuseppe Garibaldi; her many love letters from Bolivar survived only because Daniel O'Leary refused to burn his papers after his death, and after Bolivar died she was exiled and died in poverty in Peru. An 1830 congress failed to halt Venezuelan separatism, and Gran Colombia dissolved the following year, not long after Sucre's own 1830 assassination. Bolivar's legacy pervades modern Venezuelan politics: Hugo Chavez and Nicolas Maduro's socialism, the renamed "Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela," its "Bolivarian missions" (like Mission Robinson and Mission Mercal), the 1992 MRB-200 coup movement, and the ALBA trade bloc (originally his "Bolivarian Alternative") all invoke him, as do Bolivia and its currency, Venezuela's currency, a Texas peninsula, twin towers, the Bolivarian Games, a Bogota statue by Pietro Tenerani, and Ciudad Bolivar (formerly Angostura, birthplace of Angostura bitters), where he is interred at a national pantheon. He is also the title character of Gabriel Garcia Marquez's 1989 novel The General in His Labyrinth, which depicts his final deteriorating journey down the Magdalena River using his own preserved writings, and appears in works from Denzil Romero's The Wife of Dr. Thorne to John Green's Looking for Alaska.
recursive_suggestions: Manuela Saenz — has substantial independent bonus-part presence (4+ appearances as the primary answer); consider a standalone topic if her question density continues to grow
Francisco de Miranda — recurring independent answerline as "The Precursor"; distinct History topic on his own European court-canvassing career and role in the First Venezuelan Republic
The General in His Labyrinth — Gabriel Garcia Marquez novel with enough independent literary clue density (setting, supporting characters, structure) to warrant its own Literature topic
Antonio Jose de Sucre — recurring lieutenant/successor answerline (Battle of Ayacucho, Bolivia's presidency, 1830 assassination); distinct History topic
Jose Antonio Paez — recurring subordinate-turned-rival answerline ("Centaur of the Plains," first president of independent Venezuela); distinct History topic

Venezuela (modern country/Bolivarian Republic) — excluded roughly 20+ tossups/bonus parts here about Chavez, Maduro, the Bolivarian Republic, Lake Maracaibo, and current events, which belong to a separate Geography/modern-History topic
Battle of Boyaca and Battle of Carabobo — both could split into standalone Event topics if their independent clue density grows further
